title: Stable Selection Sort with Incorrect Comparison

statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, implement a stable selection sort algorithm that sorts the array in non-decreasing order. The function should return true if the array is sorted correctly and false otherwise.
statement.inputFormat: Input is an array of integers
statement.outputFormat: Return true if the array is sorted correctly and false otherwise
statement.notes: Stable selection sort is a variation of selection sort that maintains the relative order of equal elements.

examples.explanation: The array is not sorted correctly.

hints.text: The implementation of selection sort seems correct, but there might be a small mistake in the comparison.

editorial.title: Stable Selection Sort with Incorrect Comparison
editorial.intuition: The selection sort algorithm works by repeatedly finding the minimum element from the unsorted part and swapping it with the first unsorted element.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would involve iterating through the array and comparing each element with the one before it to determine the correct order.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves implementing the selection sort algorithm with a stable comparison.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& nums) { for (int i = 0; i < nums.size() - 1; ++i) { int minIndex = i; for (int j = i + 1; j < nums.size(); ++j) { if (nums[j] < nums[minIndex]) { minIndex = j; } } swap(nums[i], nums[minIndex]); } return isSorted(nums); } bool isSorted(vector<int>& nums) { for (int i = 0; i < nums.size() - 1; ++i) { if (nums[i] > nums[i + 1]) { return false; } } return true; } };
starterCode.java: class Solution { public: boolean solve(int[] nums) { for (int i = 0; i < nums.length - 1; ++i) { int minIndex = i; for (int j = i + 1; j < nums.length; ++j) { if (nums[j] < nums[minIndex]) { minIndex = j; } } swap(nums, i, minIndex); } return isSorted(nums); } boolean isSorted(int[] nums) { for (int i = 0; i < nums.length - 1; ++i) { if (nums[i] > nums[i + 1]) { return false; } } return true; } }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, nums: list[int]) -> bool: for i in range(len(nums) - 1): minIndex = i for j in range(i + 1, len(nums)): if nums[j] < nums[minIndex]: minIndex = j nums[i], nums[minIndex] = nums[minIndex], nums[i] return self.isSorted(nums) def isSorted(self, nums: list[int]) -> bool: for i in range(len(nums) - 1): if nums[i] > nums[i + 1]: return False return True
starterCode.javascript: class Solution { solve(nums) { for (let i = 0; i < nums.length - 1; ++i) { let minIndex = i; for (let j = i + 1; j < nums.length; ++j) { if (nums[j] < nums[minIndex]) { minIndex = j; } } [nums[i], nums[minIndex]] = [nums[minIndex], nums[i]]; } return this.isSorted(nums); } isSorted(nums) { for (let i = 0; i < nums.length - 1; ++i) { if (nums[i] > nums[i + 1]) { return false; } } return true; } }

solutions.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(vector<int>& nums) { for (int i = 0; i < nums.size() - 1; ++i) { int minIndex = i; for (int j = i + 1; j < nums.size(); ++j) { if (nums[j] < nums[minIndex]) { minIndex = j; } } swap(nums[i], nums[minIndex]); } return isSorted(nums); } bool isSorted(vector<int>& nums) { for (int i = 0; i < nums.size() - 1; ++i) { if (nums[i] > nums[i + 1]) { return false; } } return true; } }; int main() { int n; cin >> n; vector<int> nums(n); for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { cin >> nums[i]; } Solution solution; bool result = solution.solve(nums); cout << (result ? "true" : "false") << endl; return 0; }
